q3: The architectural rationale placeholder kept persisting — the model understood conceptually that rejected alternatives matter, but it kept either stalling for information it should have flagged earlier or filling in the placeholder with generic reasoning that didn't reflect how our system actually works. That's the one thing that didn't land cleanly across iterations.
q5: The rubric captured most of what I care about — 'Decision Rationale' and 'Preventive Information Placement' are exactly the two things I pushed back on hardest. Where it fell short is that it frames these as criteria to check rather than habits of thought, so the model would satisfy the letter of the rubric (warnings moved above code) without fully internalizing why that matters. The narrative header criterion was accurate but felt secondary given the bigger structural issues.
q6: Seeing 'Decision Rationale' as priority one made it obvious the model was pattern-matching on the concept — it knew to include rejected alternatives — but consistently defaulted to placeholders or fabricated rationale rather than flagging the information gap earlier in the conversation when it would have been cheaper to resolve. The rubric surfaced that the model's issue wasn't ignorance of the preference, it was timing: it raised the right concern too late and too repeatedly.

q3: The architectural rationale placeholder kept coming back — I flagged it multiple times and the model kept acknowledging it but never actually resolved it, just kept deferring to me to fill it in. That's the most important part of the doc and it stayed broken through three iterations.

q6: It did surface one thing: the model was consistently good at warning placement and narrative headers — those came naturally and matched what I wanted without much friction. q3: The code examples kept needing corrections — first the dead variable assignment that wouldn't compile, and before that the example was doing too much work for where it appeared in the doc. What it didn't fully capture is the code minimalism constraint — 'self-service completeness' and minimal runnable examples are actually in tension, and the rubric didn't acknowledge that tradeoff, which may be why the code examples kept running long.
q6: Seeing the rubric made me notice that the model was consistently stronger on prose structure than on code discipline — the headers and warning placement improved faster than the snippet quality did. Without the rubric labeling these as separate criteria, I might have just felt vaguely frustrated with the code without pinpointing that it was a separate failure mode from the structural issues.
